quiz.title: Unit 9 Quiz 3, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Excess Reactants and Mass of Products
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Excess Reactant Calculations, Limiting Reactants, Mass of Products from Stoichiometry, Mole Ratios

questions.question: When 10 g of Na reacts with 20 g of Cl₂, the excess reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Reaction: 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl
Molar mass Na = 23 g/mol, Cl₂ = 71 g/mol
Moles Na = 10/23 = 0.435 mol
Moles Cl₂ = 20/71 = 0.282 mol
Mole ratio Na:Cl₂ = 2:1
For 0.435 mol Na, Cl₂ needed = 0.435/2 = 0.218 mol
Available Cl₂ = 0.282 mol, which is more than needed.
Therefore, Cl₂ is the excess reactant.

questions.question: In the reaction N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃, if 1 mole of N₂ reacts with 5 moles of H₂, the excess reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Mole ratio N₂:H₂ = 1:3
For 1 mole N₂, 3 moles H₂ are required.
5 moles H₂ are available, which is more than needed.
Therefore, H₂ is the excess reactant.

questions.question: 12 g of C reacts with 64 g of O₂ to form CO₂. The excess reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Reaction: C + O₂ → CO₂
Molar mass C = 12 g/mol, O₂ = 32 g/mol
Moles C = 12/12 = 1 mol
Moles O₂ = 64/32 = 2 mol
The reaction requires a 1:1 mole ratio.
For 1 mol C, 1 mol O₂ is required. 2 mol O₂ are available.
Therefore, O₂ is the excess reactant.

questions.question: In the reaction 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO, 24 g Mg reacts with 16 g O₂. The excess reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Mg = 24 g/mol, O₂ = 32 g/mol
Moles Mg = 24/24 = 1 mol
Moles O₂ = 16/32 = 0.5 mol
Mole ratio Mg:O₂ = 2:1
For 1 mol Mg, O₂ needed = 0.5 mol
Available O₂ = 0.5 mol, exactly the amount required.
Neither reactant is in excess; both are completely consumed.

questions.question: 56 g of Fe reacts with 20 g of S to form FeS. The excess reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Reaction: Fe + S → FeS
Molar mass Fe = 56 g/mol, S = 32 g/mol
Moles Fe = 56/56 = 1 mol
Moles S = 20/32 = 0.625 mol
The reaction requires a 1:1 mole ratio.
For 1 mol Fe, 1 mol S is required. Only 0.625 mol S is available, so S is limiting.
Therefore, Fe is the excess reactant.

questions.question: In the reaction Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl₂ + H₂, if 6.5 g Zn reacts with 20 g HCl, the excess reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Zn = 65 g/mol, HCl = 36.5 g/mol
Moles Zn = 6.5/65 = 0.1 mol
Moles HCl = 20/36.5 = 0.548 mol
Mole ratio Zn:HCl = 1:2
For 0.1 mol Zn, HCl needed = 0.2 mol
Available HCl = 0.548 mol, which is more than needed.
Therefore, HCl is the excess reactant.

questions.question: 2 moles of K react with 2 moles of Br₂ to form KBr. The excess reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Reaction: 2K + Br₂ → 2KBr
Mole ratio K:Br₂ = 2:1
For 2 moles K, Br₂ needed = 1 mole
2 moles Br₂ are available, which is more than needed.
Therefore, Br₂ is the excess reactant.

questions.question: For N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃, 28.0 g N₂ and 6.0 g H₂ are combined. What mass of NH₃ is produced?
questions.explanation:
Molar mass N₂ = 28 g/mol, H₂ = 2.016 g/mol
Moles N₂ = 28/28 = 1.0 mol
Moles H₂ = 6.0/2.016 = 2.98 mol
Mole ratio N₂:H₂ = 1:3
For 1.0 mol N₂, 3.0 mol H₂ are required. Only 2.98 mol H₂ is available, so H₂ is limiting.
NH₃ produced = 2.98 × (2/3) = 1.987 mol
Molar mass NH₃ = 17.03 g/mol
Mass NH₃ = 1.987 × 17.03 = 33.8 g ≈ 34.0 g

questions.question: In 2Al + 3Cl₂ → 2AlCl₃, 5.4 g Al reacts with 21.3 g Cl₂. What mass of AlCl₃ forms?
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Al = 27 g/mol, Cl₂ = 71 g/mol
Moles Al = 5.4/27 = 0.20 mol
Moles Cl₂ = 21.3/71 = 0.30 mol
Mole ratio Al:Cl₂ = 2:3
For 0.20 mol Al, Cl₂ needed = 0.20 × (3/2) = 0.30 mol
Exactly 0.30 mol Cl₂ available — exact stoichiometry.
AlCl₃ produced = 0.20 mol
Molar mass AlCl₃ = 133.5 g/mol
Mass = 0.20 × 133.5 = 26.7 g

questions.question: For CaCO₃ + 2HCl → CaCl₂ + H₂O + CO₂, 100 g CaCO₃ and 73.0 g HCl react. What mass of CO₂ is produced?
questions.explanation:
Molar mass CaCO₃ = 100 g/mol, HCl = 36.5 g/mol
Moles CaCO₃ = 100/100 = 1.0 mol
Moles HCl = 73.0/36.5 = 2.0 mol
Mole ratio CaCO₃:HCl = 1:2
Exactly 2.0 mol HCl available — exact stoichiometry.
CO₂ produced = 1.0 mol
Molar mass CO₂ = 44.0 g/mol
Mass CO₂ = 1.0 × 44.0 = 44.0 g

questions.question: Given 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl, 11.5 g Na and 35.5 g Cl₂ react. What mass of NaCl forms?
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Na = 23 g/mol, Cl₂ = 71 g/mol
Moles Na = 11.5/23 = 0.50 mol
Moles Cl₂ = 35.5/71 = 0.50 mol
Mole ratio Na:Cl₂ = 2:1
For 0.50 mol Na, Cl₂ needed = 0.25 mol
Available Cl₂ = 0.50 mol (excess), so Na is limiting.
NaCl produced = 0.50 mol
Molar mass NaCl = 58.5 g/mol
Mass NaCl = 0.50 × 58.5 = 29.25 g ≈ 29.3 g

questions.question: For C₂H₅OH + 3O₂ → 2CO₂ + 3H₂O, 23.0 g ethanol burns with 48.0 g O₂. What mass of CO₂ forms?
questions.explanation:
Molar mass ethanol = 46 g/mol, O₂ = 32 g/mol
Moles ethanol = 23/46 = 0.50 mol
Moles O₂ = 48/32 = 1.50 mol
Mole ratio ethanol:O₂ = 1:3
Exactly 1.50 mol O₂ available — exact stoichiometry.
CO₂ produced = 0.50 × 2 = 1.0 mol
Molar mass CO₂ = 44.0 g/mol
Mass CO₂ = 1.0 × 44.0 = 44.0 g

questions.question: In 3Mg + N₂ → Mg₃N₂, 7.2 g Mg reacts with 5.6 g N₂. What mass of Mg₃N₂ forms?
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Mg = 24.3 g/mol, N₂ = 28 g/mol
Moles Mg = 7.2/24.3 = 0.296 mol
Moles N₂ = 5.6/28 = 0.20 mol
Mole ratio Mg:N₂ = 3:1
For 0.296 mol Mg, N₂ needed = 0.296/3 = 0.099 mol
Available N₂ = 0.20 mol (excess), so Mg is limiting.
Mg₃N₂ produced = 0.296/3 = 0.0987 mol
Molar mass Mg₃N₂ = 100.9 g/mol
Mass = 0.0987 × 100.9 = 9.96 g ≈ 10.0 g

questions.question: For 2Fe + 3Cl₂ → 2FeCl₃, 11.2 g Fe and 21.3 g Cl₂ react. What mass of FeCl₃ is produced?
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Fe = 55.85 g/mol, Cl₂ = 71 g/mol
Moles Fe = 11.2/55.85 = 0.201 mol
Moles Cl₂ = 21.3/71 = 0.30 mol
Mole ratio Fe:Cl₂ = 2:3
For 0.201 mol Fe, Cl₂ needed = 0.201 × (3/2) = 0.302 mol
Available Cl₂ = 0.30 mol (slightly insufficient), so Cl₂ is limiting.
FeCl₃ produced = 0.30 × (2/3) = 0.20 mol
Molar mass FeCl₃ = 162.2 g/mol
Mass = 0.20 × 162.2 = 32.44 g ≈ 32.5 g

questions.question: Given Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl₂ + H₂, 6.54 g Zn reacts with 7.30 g HCl. What mass of ZnCl₂ forms?
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Zn = 65.4 g/mol, HCl = 36.5 g/mol
Moles Zn = 6.54/65.4 = 0.100 mol
Moles HCl = 7.30/36.5 = 0.200 mol
Mole ratio Zn:HCl = 1:2
Exactly 0.200 mol HCl available — exact stoichiometry.
ZnCl₂ produced = 0.100 mol
Molar mass ZnCl₂ = 136.3 g/mol
Mass = 0.100 × 136.3 = 13.63 g ≈ 13.6 g

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered excess reactant and product mass calculations! 🧪⚖️
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review identifying limiting reactants and using mole ratios.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on converting mass to moles correctly.
scoreMessages.0: Please review stoichiometry, limiting reactants, and mass-to-mole conversions.
